questions.text: __________ represents an entity in the real world that can be distinctly identified.
questions.explanation: An object is a concrete instance of a class that represents a real-world entity with a unique identity, state, and behavior.

questions.text: _______ is a template that defines objects of the same type.
questions.explanation: A class acts as the blueprint or template that defines the structure (data fields) and characteristics (methods) for all objects created from it.

questions.text: An object is an instance of a __________.
questions.explanation: The process of creating an object is known as instantiation; therefore, an object is a specific instance of its defining class.

questions.text: The keyword __________ is required to define a class.
questions.explanation: In the Java programming language, the keyword 'class' is the mandatory syntax used to declare and define a new class block.

questions.text: If TestCircle.java in Listing 9.1 is compiled successfully, __________.
questions.explanation: When a Java source file is compiled, the compiler generates a separate .class file (bytecode) for every individual class defined within that file.

questions.text: ________ is invoked to create an object.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A method with a return type

choices.label: D
choices.text: A method with the void return type

questions.explanation: A constructor is a special type of method that is automatically invoked to initialize an object's state at the moment it is created using the 'new' operator.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiple constructors can be defined in a class.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constructors do not have a return type, not even void.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constructors must have the same name as the class itself.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constructors are invoked using the new operator when an object is created.

questions.explanation: Constructors are defined by several rules: they must match the class name exactly, they have no return type (not even void), they can be overloaded, and they are triggered by the 'new' keyword.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code.

class TempClass {
  int i;
  public void TempClass(int j) {
    int i = j;
  }
}

public class C {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    TempClass temp = new TempClass(2);
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error because TempClass does not have a default constructor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a compile error because TempClass does not have a constructor with an int argument.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program compiles fine, but it does not run because class C is not public.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program compiles and runs fine.

questions.explanation: The code contains a method named 'TempClass' (due to the void return type) rather than a constructor. Consequently, the class only has a default no-arg constructor, making the call 'new TempClass(2)' a compilation error.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A default constructor is provided automatically if no constructors are explicitly defined in the class.

choices.label: B
choices.text: At least one constructor must always be defined explicitly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every class has a default constructor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The default constructor is a no-arg constructor.

questions.explanation: A default constructor is a no-argument constructor that the compiler automatically provides only if no other constructors are explicitly defined in the class code.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    A a = new A();
    a.print();
  }
}

class A {
  String s;

  A(String s) {
    this.s = s;
  }

  void print() {
    System.out.println(s);
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error because class A is not a public class.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a compile error because class A does not have a default constructor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program compiles and runs fine and prints nothing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program would compile and run if you change A a = new A() to A a = new A("5").

questions.explanation: Because class A defines a constructor with a String parameter, the compiler does not provide a default no-arg constructor. Thus, 'new A()' causes a compile error, which could be fixed by providing a String argument.

questions.text: Given the declaration Circle x = new Circle(), which of the following statements is most accurate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: x contains an object of the Circle type.

choices.label: C
choices.text: x contains a reference to a Circle object.

choices.label: D
choices.text: You can assign an int value to x.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A reference variable is an object.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A reference variable references to an object.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A data field in a class must be of a primitive type.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A data field in a class can be of an object type.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code.

public class Test {
  int x;

  public Test(String t) {
     System.out.println("Test");
  }

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Test test = new Test();
    System.out.println(test.x);
  }
}

choices.text: The program has a compile error because System.out.println method cannot be invoked from the constructor.

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program has a compile error because you cannot create an object from the class that defines the object.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program has a compile error because Test does not have a default constructor.

Analyze the following code.

public class Test {

  public Test(String t) {
     System.out.println("Test");

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Test test = null;
    System.out.println(test.x);

}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error because test is not initialized.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a compile error because x has not been initialized.

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

choices.label: A
choices.text: Local variables do not have default values.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A variable of a primitive type holds a value of the primitive type.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A variable of a reference type holds a reference to where an object is stored in the memory.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    double radius;
    final double PI = 3.15169;
    double area = radius * radius * PI;
    System.out.println("Area is " + area);
  }
}

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program has no compile errors but will get a runtime error because radius is not initialized.

choices.text: The program compiles and runs fine.

choices.label: A
choices.text: When creating a Random object, you have to specify the seed or use the default seed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If two Random objects have the same seed, the sequence of the random numbers obtained from these two objects are identical.

questions.explanation: Explanation generation failed.

questions.text: A method that is associated with an individual object is called __________.
questions.explanation: A method that is associated with a specific object rather than the class as a whole is known as an instance method. These methods can access instance variables and other instance methods.

questions.text:
What is the output of the second println statement in the main method?

public class Foo {
  int i;
  static int s;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Foo f1 = new Foo();
    System.out.println("f1.i is " + f1.i + " f1.s is " + f1.s);
    Foo f2 = new Foo();
    System.out.println("f2.i is " + f2.i + " f2.s is " + f2.s);
    Foo f3 = new Foo();
    System.out.println("f3.i is " + f3.i + " f3.s is " + f3.s);
  }

  public Foo() {
    i++;
    s++;
  }
}
questions.explanation: In this code, 'i' is an instance variable and 's' is a static (class) variable. When f1 is created, f1.i becomes 1 and s becomes 1. When f2 is created, a new instance variable f2.i is created and initialized to 0 then incremented to 1, while the shared static variable 's' is incremented from 1 to 2.

questions.text:
What code may be filled in the blank without causing syntax or runtime errors:

public class Test {
  java.util.Date date;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Test test = new Test();
    System.out.println(_________________);
  }
}
questions.explanation: The variable 'date' is an instance variable of the Test class. To access it from the static main method, you must use an object reference (test.date). Option C would cause a NullPointerException because 'date' is null, and Options B and D fail because they try to access an instance member from a static context without a reference.

questions.text:
You should add the static keyword in the place of ? in Line ________ in the following code:

Line  1 public class Test { 
Line  2   private int age;
Line  3 
Line  4   public ? int square(int n) { 
Line  5     return n * n;
Line  6   }
Line  7
Line  8   public ? int getAge() {
Line  9     return age;
Line 10   }
Line 11 }
questions.explanation: The 'square' method only relies on the input parameter 'n' and does not access any instance variables, so it can be declared static. However, 'getAge' accesses the instance variable 'age', which requires an instance context, so it cannot be static.

questions.text: To declare a constant MAX_LENGTH as a member of the class, you write
questions.explanation: To declare a constant in a class, you use the 'final' keyword to make it unchangeable and the 'static' keyword so it belongs to the class itself. Option E correctly uses 'final static double' followed by the variable name and value.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code.

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int n = 2;
    xMethod(n);
           
    System.out.println("n is " + n);
  }

  void xMethod(int n) {
    n++;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code has a compile error because xMethod does not return a value.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code has a compile error because xMethod is not declared static.

questions.explanation: The 'main' method is a static method. Static methods cannot call instance methods (like xMethod, which lacks the 'static' keyword) directly; they must call them through an object reference. This results in a compile-time error.

questions.text:
What is the output of the third println statement in the main method?

public class Foo {
  int i;
  static int s;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Foo f1 = new Foo();
    System.out.println("f1.i is " + f1.i + " f1.s is " + f1.s);
    Foo f2 = new Foo();
    System.out.println("f2.i is " + f2.i + " f2.s is " + f2.s);
    Foo f3 = new Foo();
    System.out.println("f3.i is " + f3.i + " f3.s is " + f3.s);
  }

  public Foo() {
    i++;
    s++;
  }
}
questions.explanation: A constructor is an instance context. From within an instance context, you can call both static methods (class-level) and other instance methods (object-level) of the same class.

questions.text:
Suppose the xMethod() is invoked in the following constructor in a class, xMethod() is _________ in the class.

public MyClass() {
  xMethod();
}

choices.label: C
choices.text: a static method or an instance method

questions.explanation: Because the 'main' method is static, it can only call other static methods in the same class directly. To call an instance method, it would require an instance of the class (an object) to be created first.

questions.text:
Suppose the xMethod() is invoked from a main method in a class as follows, xMethod() is _________ in the class.

public static void main(String[] args) {
  xMethod();
}

choices.label: C
choices.text: a static method or an instance method

questions.explanation: Variables declared with the 'static' modifier are known as class variables or static variables. They are shared by all instances of the class, meaning there is only one copy of the variable regardless of how many objects are created.

questions.text: Variables that are shared by every instance of a class are __________.
questions.explanation: Class variables (also known as static variables) are those that are shared across every instance of a class. Unlike instance variables, which are unique to each object, class variables exist in a single memory location associated with the class itself.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String args[]) {
    NClass nc = new NClass();
    nc.t = nc.t++;
  }
}

class NClass {
  int t;
  private NClass() {
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error because the NClass class has a private constructor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program does not compile because the parameter list of the main method is wrong.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program compiles, but has a runtime error because t has no initial value.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program compiles and runs fine.

questions.explanation: The class NClass has a private constructor, which means it cannot be instantiated from any code outside of the NClass class itself. Because the Test class attempts to create an instance of NClass using 'new NClass()', the compiler will throw an error regarding access privileges.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  private int t;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x;
    System.out.println(t);
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The variable t is not initialized and therefore causes errors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The variable t is private and therefore cannot be accessed in the main method.

choices.label: C
choices.text: t is non-static and it cannot be referenced in a static context in the main method.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The variable x is not initialized and therefore causes errors.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The program compiles and runs fine.

questions.explanation: In Java, a static method (like main) cannot access non-static instance variables (like t) directly. This is because instance variables require a specific object instance to exist, whereas static methods belong to the class itself and can be called without any object reference.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code and choose the best answer:

public class Foo {
  private int x;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Foo foo = new Foo();
    System.out.println(foo.x);
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Since x is private, it cannot be accessed from an object foo.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Since x is defined in the class Foo, it can be accessed by any method inside the class without using an object. You can write the code to access x without creating an object such as foo in this code.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Since x is an instance variable, it cannot be directly used inside a main method. However, it can be accessed through an object such as foo in this code.

choices.label: D
choices.text: You cannot create a self-referenced object; that is, foo is created inside the class Foo.

questions.explanation: Private members are accessible within the class they are defined in. While an instance variable like x cannot be accessed directly in a static method (like main) without an object reference, it is perfectly legal to access it via an object reference (foo.x) within the same class Foo.

questions.text: To prevent a class from being instantiated, _____________________

choices.label: A
choices.text: don't use any modifiers on the constructor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: use the public modifier on the constructor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: use the private modifier on the constructor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: use the static modifier on the constructor.

questions.explanation: By declaring the constructor of a class as private, you restrict access so that no other class can instantiate it. This is a common technique used in utility classes containing only static methods or in the Singleton design pattern.

questions.text: Which is the advantage of encapsulation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Making the class final causes no consequential changes to other code.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It enables changes to the implementation without changing a class's contract and causes no consequential changes to other code.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It enables changes to a class's contract without changing the implementation and causes no consequential changes to other code.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides the internal data and implementation details. This decoupling allows developers to change how the class works internally (the implementation) without changing the external interface (the contract), ensuring that existing code using the class does not need to be updated.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use the private modifier to encapsulate data fields.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encapsulating data fields makes the program easy to maintain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encapsulating data fields makes the program short.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encapsulating data fields helps prevent programming errors.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation involves using the private modifier for data fields (A) and providing public methods to access them. This practice helps prevent accidental data corruption or unauthorized access (D), which ultimately makes the code more robust and easier to maintain (B).

questions.text: Suppose you wish to provide an accessor method for a boolean property finished, what should the signature of this method?
questions.explanation: According to standard Java naming conventions (JavaBeans), the accessor method for a boolean property should be prefixed with 'is' instead of 'get'. Therefore, for a property named 'finished', the standard signature is 'public boolean isFinished()'.

questions.text: Encapsulation means ______________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: that data fields should be declared private

choices.label: B
choices.text: that a class can extend another class

choices.label: C
choices.text: that a variable of supertype can refer to a subtype object

choices.label: D
choices.text: that a class can contain another class

questions.explanation: Encapsulation is the core object-oriented principle of bundling data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (a class) and restricting direct access to the internal state, typically by declaring data fields as private.

questions.text:
What is the value of myCount.count displayed?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Count myCount = new Count();
    int times = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
      increment(myCount, times);

    System.out.println(
      "myCount.count = " + myCount.count);
    System.out.println("times = "+ times);
  }

  public static void increment(Count c, int times) {
    c.count++;
    times++;
  }
}

class Count {
  int count;

  Count(int c) {
    count = c;
  }

  Count() {
    count = 1;
  }
}
questions.explanation: The Count object is initialized with a count of 1 via its no-arg constructor. In Java, objects are passed by reference value, meaning the increment method modifies the actual object. Since the loop runs 100 times and increments the count each time, the final value is 1 + 100 = 101.

questions.text:
What is the value of times displayed?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Count myCount = new Count();
    int times = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
      increment(myCount, times);

    System.out.println(
      "myCount.count = " + myCount.count);
    System.out.println("times = "+ times);
  }

  public static void increment(Count c, int times) {
    c.count++;
    times++;
  }
}

class Count {
  int count;

  Count(int c) {
    count = c;
  }

  Count() {
    count = 1;
  }
}
questions.explanation: In Java, primitive types like 'int' are passed by value. When the variable 'times' is passed to the increment method, a local copy is created. Incrementing this copy inside the method does not affect the original 'times' variable in the main method, so its value remains 0.

questions.text:
What is the output of the following program?

import java.util.Date;

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Date date = new Date(1234567);
    m1(date);
    System.out.print(date.getTime() + " ");

    m2(date);
    System.out.println(date.getTime());
  }

  public static void m1(Date date) {
    date = new Date(7654321);
  }

  public static void m2(Date date) {
    date.setTime(7654321);
  }
}
questions.explanation: In Java, arguments are passed by value. For object types, the value passed is the reference to the object. In m1, reassigning the 'date' parameter to a new object only changes the local copy of the reference, not the original reference in main. In m2, the 'setTime' method modifies the actual state of the object that the reference points to, so the change is reflected in main.

questions.text: When invoking a method with an object argument, ___________ is passed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the object is copied, then the reference of the copied object

questions.explanation: In Java, when an object is passed as an argument to a method, the method receives a copy of the reference (memory address) to that object. Both the original reference and the method's parameter now point to the same object in memory.

questions.text: Given the declaration Circle[] x = new Circle[10], which of the following statements is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: x contains an array of ten int values.

choices.label: B
choices.text: x contains an array of ten objects of the Circle type.

choices.label: C
choices.text: x contains a reference to an array and each element in the array can hold a reference to a Circle object.

choices.label: D
choices.text: x contains a reference to an array and each element in the array can hold a Circle object.

questions.explanation: The statement 'new Circle[10]' allocates memory for an array capable of holding 10 references to Circle objects. The variable 'x' holds a reference to this array structure. Initially, every element in the array is initialized to the default value for references, which is null.

questions.text: Assume java.util.Date[] dates = new java.util.Date[10], which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: C
choices.text: dates = new java.util.Date[5] is fine, which assigns a new array to dates.

choices.label: D
choices.text: dates = new Date() is fine, which creates a new Date object and assigns to dates.

questions.explanation: The statement 'new java.util.Date[10]' creates an array object and assigns its reference to 'dates', so 'dates' is not null. However, the elements within an array of objects are initialized to null by default, so 'dates[0]' is null. Additionally, since 'dates' is a variable of type 'Date[]', it can be reassigned to point to another array object of the same type.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true about an immutable object?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The contents of an immutable object cannot be modified.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All properties of an immutable object must be private.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All properties of an immutable object must be of primitive types.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A readable object type property in an immutable object must also be immutable.

choices.label: E
choices.text: An immutable object contains no mutator methods.

questions.explanation: An immutable object must have its state fixed upon creation. This requires that contents cannot be modified (A), properties are private to prevent direct access (B), and no mutator methods exist (E). If an object has a reference to a mutable object, it could potentially be changed, so properties that are object types must also be immutable (D). Primitive types are not a requirement (C).

questions.text:
What is the output for the first print statement in the main method?

public class Foo {
  static int i = 0;
  static int j = 0;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int i = 2;
    int k = 3;
    {
      int j = 3;
      System.out.println("i + j is " + i + j);
    }

    k = i + j;
    System.out.println("k is " + k);
    System.out.println("j is " + j);
  }
}
questions.explanation: The expression '"i + j is " + i + j' is evaluated from left to right. First, the string is concatenated with the integer 'i' (2), resulting in the string 'i + j is 2'. Then, that string is concatenated with the integer 'j' (3), resulting in 'i + j is 23'.

questions.text:
What is the output for the second print statement in the main method?

public class Foo {
  static int i = 0;
  static int j = 0;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int i = 2;
    int k = 3;
    {
      int j = 3;
      System.out.println("i + j is " + i + j);
    }

    k = i + j;
    System.out.println("k is " + k);
    System.out.println("j is " + j);
  }
}
questions.explanation: The variable 'k' is assigned the result of 'i + j'. In this scope, 'i' refers to the local variable (2). However, the local 'j' defined in the inner block is no longer in scope, so 'j' refers to the static class variable, which is initialized to 0. Thus, 'k = 2 + 0 = 2'.

questions.text:
What is the output for the third print statement in the main method?

public class Foo {
  static int i = 0;
  static int j = 0;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int i = 2;
    int k = 3;
    {
      int j = 3;
      System.out.println("i + j is " + i + j);
    }

    k = i + j;
    System.out.println("k is " + k);
    System.out.println("j is " + j);
  }
}
questions.explanation: When 'System.out.println("j is " + j)' is called, the local variable 'j' declared in the inner curly-brace block is no longer accessible because its scope has ended. Therefore, the compiler looks for 'j' in the outer scopes and finds the static class variable 'j', which is 0.

questions.text: You can declare two variables with the same name in __________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: a method, one as a formal parameter and the other as a local variable

choices.label: C
choices.text: two nested blocks in a method (two nested blocks means one being inside the other)

questions.explanation: In Java, you can use the same variable name for local variables in different methods because their scopes are independent. However, you cannot have a local variable and a formal parameter with the same name in the same method, nor can you declare a variable in an inner block with the same name as a variable in the enclosing method scope.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

class Circle {
  private double radius;
  
  public Circle(double radius) {
    radius = radius;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error because it does not have a main method.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program will compile, but you cannot create an object of Circle with a specified radius. The object will always have radius 0.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program has a compile error because you cannot assign radius to radius.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program does not compile because Circle does not have a default constructor.

questions.explanation: The code 'radius = radius' inside the constructor is an assignment of the parameter 'radius' to itself. Because the parameter name shadows the instance variable name, the instance variable 'this.radius' is never updated and retains its default value of 0.0.

questions.text:
Which of the following can be placed in the blank line in the following code?

public class Test {
  private int id;
  
  public void m1() {
    _____.id = 45;
  }
}
questions.explanation: In Java, the keyword 'this' refers to the current instance of the class. In the instance method 'm1()', 'this.id' correctly identifies the instance variable 'id'. 'Test' would be used for static members, 'self' is used in other languages like Python but not Java, and 'This' is invalid because Java is case-sensitive.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

class Test {
  private double i;
  
  public Test(double i) {
    this.t();
    this.i = i;
  }

  public Test() {
    System.out.println("Default constructor");
    this(1);
  }

  public void t() {
    System.out.println("Invoking t");
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: this.t() may be replaced by t().

choices.label: B
choices.text: this.i may be replaced by i.

choices.label: C
choices.text: this(1) must be called before System.out.println("Default constructor").

choices.label: D
choices.text: this(1) must be replaced by this(1.0).

questions.explanation: This question has two correct answers. Statement A is correct because the 'this' prefix is optional when calling an instance method within the same class unless there is a naming conflict. Statement C is correct because Java syntax rules mandate that a call to another constructor (constructor chaining via 'this()') must be the very first statement in a constructor.
